From: Sean <sean.lead@tc.gc.ca>
To: Jason Hao <jason.hao@tc.gc.ca>, Roger <roger.sr@tc.gc.ca>
Date: Mon, 11 May 2026 09:15:00 -0400
Subject: Preliminary Inquiry: NOP Risk Score Model Efficacy


Jason, Roger,

I have received several informal reports from Senior Inspectors indicating that the current Risk Score model is failing to identify high-risk sites accurately in the 2026 planning cycle. 

Before I escalate this to Carolyn, I need a data-backed assessment of the following:
1. The historical accuracy rate of the 2015 model based on non-compliance findings over the last two fiscal years.
2. The technical feasibility of integrating dynamic accident data into our current SAS architecture.

Please provide a brief technical summary by Wednesday EOD.

structured_fields.subject: Preliminary Inquiry: NOP Risk Score Model Efficacy

author: Sean

raw_text:
From: Jason Hao <jason.hao@tc.gc.ca>
To: Sean <sean.lead@tc.gc.ca>
Cc: Roger <roger.sr@tc.gc.ca>
Date: Tue, 12 May 2026 14:30:00 -0400
Subject: RE: Preliminary Inquiry: NOP Risk Score Model Efficacy


Sean,

Roger and I have completed a cross-reference between the 2015 model scores and actual inspection outcomes for FY 2024-2025. 

Key Findings:
- False Negatives: 14% of sites categorized as 'Low Risk' by the model were subsequently found to have 'Major' or 'Critical' non-compliance issues during random spot checks.
- SAS Constraints: The legacy PROC SQL joins are currently hard-coded for annual static imports. Moving to a dynamic model will require a significant re-engineering of the data pipeline.

We recommend a formal re-evaluation of the NOP Risk Score algorithm to mitigate regulatory gaps.

structured_fields.subject: RE: Preliminary Inquiry: NOP Risk Score Model Efficacy

Date: Wed, 13 May 2026 10:00:00 -0400
Subject: Formal Proposal: Re-evaluation of National Oversight Plan (NOP) Risk Score Model


Carolyn,

Based on a technical audit conducted by Jason and Roger (attached), it has become evident that our current 2015 Risk Score model is no longer meeting NOP accuracy thresholds. 

Proposed Action Plan:
1. Initiate a formal 'Risk Score Re-evaluation' project for FY 2026-27.
2. Secure budget for external consulting to support predictive modeling.
3. Modernize the underlying SAS data architecture to support dynamic risk factors.

This modernization is essential to ensure our inspection resources are allocated to the highest-threat sites as per TDG mandates.

Carolyn, Sean,

I am approving the initiation of the Risk Score Re-evaluation project in principle. Given the increasing complexity of dangerous goods transport, we cannot rely on outdated metrics.

However, I want to emphasize that the final output must be 'Auditable.' Whatever algorithm we implement must be transparent enough to defend during a Parliamentary audit.

Jason, I need you to prepare a 'Data Dictionary' and a sample anonymized dataset from the TDG_CORE database for their initial review. We will have the kick-off meeting next Tuesday to align on the technical roadmap.

To: Sean <sean.lead@tc.gc.ca>, Jason Hao <jason.hao@tc.gc.ca>

Date: Mon, 01 Jun 2026 10:30:00 -0400
Subject: Kick-off Follow-up: Initial Data Requirements List


Sean, Jason,

Thank you for the productive kick-off. To build the predictive model discussed, we require the following data dimensions per site:
1. Historical inspection non-compliance severity (past 5 years).
2. Real-time proximity to Emergency Response Assistance Plan (ERAP) centers.
3. Corporate financial stability ratings (to assess maintenance investment risk).
4. Total annual shipment volume of Class 1 and Class 7 dangerous goods.

Jason, please confirm the availability of these fields in your SAS environment so we can begin the weighting exercise.

author: Dr. Aris

raw_text:
From: Jason Hao <jason.hao@tc.gc.ca>

Date: Tue, 02 Jun 2026 15:20:00 -0400
Subject: RE: Kick-off Follow-up: Initial Data Requirements List


Dr. Aris,

I have reviewed the data requirements against our current SAS production schemas. Please note the following constraints:
- Item 2: Our database only contains static postal codes for sites. Real-time proximity mapping is not supported by the current architecture.
- Item 3: Corporate financial stability is not a field captured under TDG mandates. We do not have access to this third-party data.
- Item 4: Volume data is self-reported and often contains significant missing values for smaller operators.

We may need to discuss 'Proxy Indicators' to compensate for these data gaps during our next sync.

structured_fields.subject: RE: Kick-off Follow-up: Initial Data Requirements List

author: Jason Hao

raw_text:
From: "Dr. Aris" <aris.consultant@ext.tc.gc.ca>

Date: Wed, 03 Jun 2026 09:10:00 -0400
Subject: RE: RE: Kick-off Follow-up: Initial Data Requirements List


Jason,

Understood regarding the postal codes, we can use a lookup table for centroids as a workaround. However, excluding financial stability (Item 3) will lower the model's predictive power for proactive maintenance risk.

Can we explore using 'Facility Age' or 'Parent Company Size' as a proxy for financial stability? I would like to see the correlation coefficients for these fields in your baseline data before we finalize the model structure.

Subject: Internal Note: Historical context on Facility Age data


Jason, Sean,

Just a heads-up before you respond to Aris: be careful with 'Facility Age'. During the 2018 migration, we found that nearly 25% of the entries in that column were reset to '1900-01-01' due to a system glitch. 

I suggest Jason runs a frequency check on that field before we commit to it as a proxy. If the data quality is too poor, we should push back on using it in the final weighting logic.

author: Roger

Cc: Sean <sean.lead@tc.gc.ca>, Roger <roger.sr@tc.gc.ca>
Date: Mon, 08 Jun 2026 11:20:00 -0400
Subject: Preliminary Correlation Results: Facility Age as Proxy


Dr. Aris,

Following up on our discussion regarding proxy indicators, I ran a correlation analysis between 'Facility Age' and historical 'Major Violations' using the last 3 fiscal years of data. 

As Roger anticipated, the data quality in the 'Facility Age' field is inconsistent. After filtering out the systemic defaults (1900-01-01), the Pearson correlation coefficient was only 0.12, which is statistically insignificant for our weighting purposes. 

I recommend we revert to 'Past Inspection Performance' as the primary risk driver and omit the financial proxy entirely to maintain model integrity.

Subject: Concerns regarding Draft Risk Model - Field Perspective


Sean, Jason,

I have reviewed the preliminary site rankings generated by the draft model. I am concerned that several high-priority chemical transload facilities in the Vancouver port area have been downgraded to 'Medium' or 'Low' risk. 

While their 'Transport Volume' might be lower than some rural hubs, their proximity to high-density residential areas is not being adequately captured. From an enforcement standpoint, if we reduce inspection frequency for these sites based solely on this algorithm, we are creating a significant public safety gap.

We need to discuss the inclusion of a 'Geographic Sensitivity' override before this goes to the pilot stage.

Aris, Jason,

I have reviewed the v1 table, and I have major reservations. Several high-volume transload facilities in the Vancouver corridor are still showing as 'Low Risk' under this 10% weighting. This does not reflect the operational reality we are seeing on the ground. We need a v2 that prioritizes high-density urban hubs more aggressively.

Thank you for the feedback. The current model uses a standardized 'Urban vs. Rural' classification which accounts for 5% of the total score. Increasing this weight significantly might dilute the impact of the core 'Compliance History' metrics, which are the strongest predictors of future incidents. 

Jason, is it possible to extract more granular GIS data (e.g., proximity to schools/hospitals) for the Vancouver area to see if it justifies a specific regional override without compromising the national logic?

Sean, Dr. Aris,

I have completed the sensitivity analysis as requested. I ran the model through the full national dataset using three weighting scenarios for 'Geographic Sensitivity':

1. Baseline (5%): No change to current draft.
2. Scenario A (10%): Increases urban site priority; shifts ~120 inspections from rural to urban hubs.
3. Scenario B (15%): Recovers all Vancouver 'False Negatives' identified by Dave, but increases total national oversight hours by 4.2% due to travel logistics.

Attached is the 'Impact_Summary_Table.xlsx'. This data should provide the basis for Marc's decision on Wednesday.

Marc, Carolyn,

Following today's review meeting, we have reached a consensus on the final weighting logic for the FY 2026-27 Risk Model.

Decision Points:
- We will adopt Scenario B (15% Geographic Sensitivity) to address safety concerns in high-density urban areas.
- To offset the 4.2% increase in oversight hours, we will implement a 'Low-Risk Streamlining' protocol for sites with 5+ years of perfect compliance.
- Dr. Aris will finalize the algorithm documentation by Friday.

Jason, please proceed with the final SAS production script based on these parameters.

Cc: Roger <roger.sr@tc.gc.ca>
Date: Thu, 18 Jun 2026 09:15:00 -0400
Subject: Technical Note: Implementation of Low-Risk Streamlining in SAS


Sean,

Regarding the decision to implement 'Low-Risk Streamlining' as an offset: I am adding a boolean flag `STREAMLINE_ELIGIBLE` to the master script. 

Logic: If `YRS_SINCE_VIOLATION >= 5` AND `TOTAL_SCORE <= 20`, the inspection frequency is halved. This effectively brings our total NOP hours back to the 2025-26 baseline. Roger is currently peer-reviewing the SQL joins to ensure we don't accidentally filter out sites that haven't been inspected at all in that period.

TRANSPORT CANADA - STRATEGIC OVERSIGHT DIRECTORATE
MEETING MINUTES: NOP RISK MODEL MODERNIZATION KICKOFF

DATE: June 05, 2026
TIME: 10:00 AM - 11:30 AM
LOCATION: Boardroom 4B / MS Teams (Hybrid)
CHAIR: Marc (Director)
RECORDED BY: Aris (Consultant)

[ATTENDEES]
- Marc (Director, Strategic Oversight)
- Sean (Project Lead)
- Jason Hao (Technical Lead)
- Dr. Aris (Lead Statistical Consultant)
- Dave (Regional Representative - Pacific, via Teams)
- Judy (Manager, Data Unit)

[AGENDA]
1. Review of the 2015 Legacy Risk Model limitations.
2. Presentation of Scenario A (Proposed by Dr. Aris).
3. Technical feasibility of the TDG_CORE database migration.

[DISCUSSION]
- 1.1. OPENING: Marc emphasized that the 2026-27 National Oversight Plan (NOP) must be budget-neutral. Any increase in inspection frequency in one area must be offset by efficiencies elsewhere.
- 1.2. SCENARIO A PRESENTATION: Dr. Aris proposed a 10% Geographic Sensitivity weight. He argued that statistical variance from 2015-2025 shows geography is a secondary risk factor compared to Volume and Compliance history.
- 1.3. REGIONAL PUSHBACK: Dave (Pacific) immediately challenged the 10% figure. He cited the recent surge in Vancouver port activity (Ref: Port congestion reports) and argued that 10% still leaves high-density urban corridors "dangerously under-sampled."
- 1.4. TECHNICAL CONCERNS: Jason Hao noted that the current TDG_CORE master table contains significant "legacy decay," particularly in facility registration dates. Jason cautioned that applying any new weights before data cleaning would lead to skewed results.

[ACTION ITEMS]
- ACTION 001: Dr. Aris to refine Scenario A and provide a formal methodology draft by June 12. (Owner: Aris)
- ACTION 002: Jason Hao to perform an initial SQL scan of the 'FACILITY_AGE' fields and report on data hygiene status. (Owner: Jason)

DOCUMENT TITLE: TDG Predictive Risk Model - Statistical Methodology
DOCUMENT ID: TC-TDG-ARIS-001-DRAFT
AUTHOR: Dr. Aris (Lead Consultant)
DATE: June 05, 2026
STATUS: PRELIMINARY DRAFT

1. MATHEMATICAL FRAMEWORK
The National Oversight Plan (NOP) Risk Model is based on a weighted linear regression of three primary risk vectors:
    R = (W1 * V) + (W2 * C) + (W3 * G)
Where:
    V = Volume (Tonnage handled per annum)
    C = Compliance (Historical violation count)
    G = Geography (Regional incident density)

2. PROPOSED WEIGHTING (SCENARIO A)
Based on national historical variance from 2015-2025, the recommended distribution is:
    - Volume Weight (W1): 0.40
    - Compliance Weight (W2): 0.50
    - Geographic Weight (W3): 0.10

3. STATISTICAL JUSTIFICATION
A 10.0% geographic weight represents the maximum statistically defensible adjustment from the 2015 baseline (5%) without introducing unjustified regional bias. Further increases in geographic weighting risk reducing the predictive power of long-term compliance history in favour of proximity-based assumptions.

4. KNOWN LIMITATIONS
This draft assumes 100% data cleanliness of the TDG_CORE database and does not account for inspector-driven manual overrides.
author: Dr. Aris

    R = (0.35 * V) + (0.50 * C) + (0.15 * G)

2. TECHNICAL ENHANCEMENTS (DATA HYGIENE)
Section 2.1: Legacy Decay Mitigation
Analysis of the TDG_CORE master table revealed 11,240 records with a default FACILITY_AGE_DATE of '1900-01-01'. These records represent 'Technical Debt' from the 2018 database migration. 
- Implementation: The SAS macro %FILTER_DECAY was developed to exclude these records from the baseline age-calculation, preventing artificial inflation of risk scores for dormant sites.

3. GEOGRAPHIC SENSITIVITY (THE 15% RULE)
To address the 'Vancouver Gap' identified by regional inspectors, the Geographic Sensitivity (G) weight was increased from 5.0% to 15.0%.
- Calculation: Facilities located within 50km of a Census Metropolitan Area (CMA) boundary receive a fixed 100-point boost to their 'G' vector, multiplied by the 0.15 weight.

4. THE STRL-2026 (STREAMLINING) PROTOCOL
To balance the increased inspection load created by the 15% geographic weight, the 'Streamlining' filter was introduced:
- Logic: If (Years_Since_Violation >= 5) AND (Total_Risk_Score < 20), the facility is eligible for reduced inspection frequency.
- Safeguard: Facilities flagged with 'ERAP_FLAG = 1' (High Hazard) are categorically excluded from streamlining, regardless of compliance history.

5. CONCLUSION
This model (Scenario B) achieves a 100% capture rate of high-volume urban hubs while remaining within the national FTE (Full-Time Equivalent) budget.

REPORT ID: TC-TDG-2026-CCR-001
TITLE: Comprehensive Conflict Validation Report: Automated Streamlining vs. High-Risk Safety Indicators
PREPARED BY: Jason Hao, Technical Lead (Data Analytics)
DATE: July 05, 2026
DISTRIBUTION: Marc (Director), Sean (Lead), Carolyn (Manager), Internal Audit Division

1. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
In accordance with the NOP Modernization Directive (2026), this report outlines the validation of the 'Low-Risk Streamlining' (STRL-2026) filter. The objective was to ensure that the automation of inspection deferrals for low-risk entities did not create a 'safety gap' by inadvertently de-prioritizing facilities with latent risk factors not captured by the primary score.

2. LOGIC DEFINITION & PARAMETERS
The STRL-2026 algorithm utilizes a multi-factor exclusion join. A facility is only eligible for the 'Streamlined' status (reduced inspection frequency) if it meets the following exhaustive criteria:
  2.1. HISTORICAL COMPLIANCE: No 'Critical' or 'Major' violations recorded in the TC Enforcement Database for the preceding 60 months.
  2.2. RISK THRESHOLD: A calculated Total Risk Score of <20 (on a 100-point scale).
  2.3. INCIDENT STABILITY: Zero 'Reportable Incidents' (as defined under Part 8 of the TDG Regulations) in the past 3 fiscal years.
  2.4. ERAP EXEMPTION: The facility does not handle materials requiring an Emergency Response Assistance Plan (ERAP).

3. VALIDATION METHODOLOGY (STRESS TESTING)
The technical team performed a 'Reverse-Stress Test' using PROC SQL. We merged the proposed 'Streamline_Eligible' list against the 'Inspector-Flagged Priority List' (a manual list of 120 urban facilities provided by regional offices, including Dave’s Vancouver corridor).
  - Primary Key: TC_FACILITY_ID
  - Comparison Variable: GEO_SENSITIVITY_OVERRIDE (15%)

4. QUANTITATIVE FINDINGS
  - Total Facility Universe: 45,312 records.
  - Streamlining Initial Candidates: 3,580 records.
  - Post-Conflict Check Filtering: 160 records were REJECTED from streamlining because they hit the 15% Geographic Sensitivity threshold (Vancouver/Toronto/Montreal corridors).
  - Net Streamlined Population: 3,420 records.
  - Conflict Hits: 0. There is no overlap between the final Streamlined list and the High-Risk Urban list.

5. QUALITY ASSURANCE CONCLUSION
The implementation of the 15% Geographic Sensitivity weight acts as a 'Safety Valve.' Even if a facility in a high-density area has 5 years of perfect compliance, the new weight ensures it remains in the active oversight queue. I certify that the STRL-2026 logic is robust and compliant with the 2026 Safety Mandate.

[ELECTRONICALLY SIGNED]
Jason Hao, Technical Lead

ENVIRONMENT AND CLIMATE CHANGE CANADA (ECCC)
METEOROLOGICAL APPLICATIONS & INDUSTRIAL SAFETY DIVISION

DOCUMENT ID: ECCC-2026-TB-088
DATE: DECEMBER 20, 2026
SUBJECT: IMPACT ANALYSIS OF THE DECEMBER 2026 'POLAR VORTEX' ON INDUSTRIAL INFRASTRUCTURE IN THE QUEBEC-ONTARIO CORRIDOR

1. METEOROLOGICAL SUMMARY
Between December 10 and December 18, 2026, the Southwestern Ontario and St. Lawrence corridors experienced an unprecedented 'Thermal Cycling' event. Data from Pearson (YYZ) and Trudeau (YUL) weather stations recorded temperature swings of up to 30°C within 24-hour cycles (ranging from a high of +2°C during daylight hours to a nocturnal low of -32°C).

2. TECHNICAL IMPACT ON PRESSURE RETAINING COMPONENTS
Environmental engineers at ECCC have analyzed the impact of these rapid expansion and contraction cycles on industrial sealing materials. 

2.1. ELASTOMERIC SEAL DEGRADATION
Standard nitrile and viton gaskets used in legacy transportation equipment (specifically Type-111 and legacy DOT-111 tank cars) are rated for consistent cold, but rapid thermal cycling induces "material fatigue." This leads to micro-fissures in valve seals and manway gaskets.

2.2. VALVE SEEPAGE RISKS
Our findings indicate that Class 3 (Flammable Liquids) are particularly susceptible to 'micro-seepage' under these conditions. The drop in ambient temperature causes a reduction in internal tank pressure, followed by a rapid increase during daytime warming, which can bypass compromised seals.

3. GEOGRAPHIC RISK CORRELATION
The highest risk density from this specific event is identified in the "Windsor-Quebec City Corridor," where the concentration of industrial rail yards is highest. We have observed a 14.5% increase in 'Non-Accidental Releases' (NARs) reported via satellite thermal sensors in rail marshaling yards during this 8-day period.

Secondary Risk Zone - Prairie Region: While the December 2026 polar vortex was centred on the Ontario-Quebec corridor, ECCC meteorological data indicates that the Prairie provinces (Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba) have experienced persistent sub-standard temperature cycling throughout Q4 2026 and into Q1 2027. Legacy infrastructure in Prairie rail marshaling yards — particularly Type-111 tank cars in high-volume Class 3 transfer hubs — should be considered at elevated risk of cumulative thermal fatigue in the coming months. Federal regulators are advised to monitor Class 3 incident frequencies in the Prairie region through Q2 2027.

4. ADVISORY TO FEDERAL REGULATORS
It is strongly recommended that federal oversight bodies (e.g., Transport Canada) initiate a high-frequency inspection protocol for facilities handling Class 3 Flammable Liquids in affected regions. Priority should be given to the Windsor-Quebec City Corridor as the immediate acute risk zone, with the Prairie region flagged for proactive monitoring through mid-2027. Special attention should be given to facilities utilizing legacy rolling stock, as these are the primary failure points identified in this thermal event.

--------------------------------------------------
PREPARED BY: Dr. L. Henderson, Senior Climatologist
VERIFIED BY: Industrial Hazards Branch, ECCC
--------------------------------------------------

1. PURPOSE
This guideline provides the framework for Internal Audit (IAD) to evaluate the integrity, transparency, and defensibility of algorithmic models used in the National Oversight Plan (NOP).

2. CORE AUDIT CRITERIA (THE "FOUR PILLARS")

   2.1. PILLAR 1: DATA LINEAGE AND HYGIENE
   Auditors must verify the 'cleanliness' of input data. The technical leads must demonstrate:
   - Identification and isolation of legacy data artifacts (e.g., '1900-01-01' default dates from 20th-century COBOL migrations).
   - Documented rationale for data exclusion or filtering.
   - Verification of SAS or SQL logs (raw execution files) to confirm code matches the methodology.

   2.2. PILLAR 2: WEIGHTING DEFENSIBILITY
   Any shift in model weights (e.g., Geographic vs. Volume) must be supported by:
   - A formal Sensitivity Analysis demonstrating the impact of the change.
   - Evidence of stakeholder feedback (e.g., input from regional inspection teams).
   - Alignment with current external risk markers (e.g., Port traffic surges, environmental factors).

   2.3. PILLAR 3: ADMINISTRATIVE TRACEABILITY
   The audit division requires a 'paper trail' for all production deployments. This must include:
   - Formal Decision Memos signed by a Director-level official (EX-01) authorizing parameter changes.
   - Clear version control (e.g., V1.0 Aris Draft vs. V2.0 Hao Final).

   2.4. PILLAR 4: RISK MITIGATION (CONFLICT CHECKING)
   If efficiency filters (such as 'Streamlining' or 'Deferred Inspections') are implemented, the auditor must perform a reverse-check:
   - Ensure that facilities handling High-Hazard materials (ERAP-required) are never bypassed by automated streamlining logic.

3. AUDIT RATING SCALE
- Fully Compliant: Evidence provided for all 4 Pillars.
- Partially Compliant: Deficiencies in documentation or traceability.
- Non-Compliant: Critical lack of audit trail or unauthorized parameter changes.

4. REPORTING PROTOCOL
Initial findings will be communicated via 'Audit Memo' to the Directorate of Strategic Oversight. Final status will be logged in the departmental Audit Tracking System.

DIRECTIVE ON AUTOMATED DECISION-MAKING

FILE REFERENCE: TBS-DIGITAL-2024-AD-009
EFFECTIVE DATE: APRIL 01, 2024
SECURITY CLASSIFICATION: UNCLASSIFIED

1. OBJECTIVE
The objective of this Directive is to ensure that Automated Decision Systems (ADS) are deployed in a manner that reduces risk to Canadians, ensures federal institutions are accountable, and aligns with the core values of the public sector.

2. SCOPE
This Directive applies to any system, tool, or statistical model used to assist or replace the judgment of human decision-makers in administrative processes (e.g., scoring for inspection frequency).

3. MANDATORY REQUIREMENTS
   3.1. ALGORITHMIC IMPACT ASSESSMENT (AIA)
   Institutions must complete and document an AIA prior to the production rollout of any high-stakes scoring model to identify potential safety gaps.

   3.2. HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP (HITL)
   For systems determining the frequency of oversight or enforcement (e.g., TDG National Oversight Plan), a human officer must have the authority to override automated outputs based on regional intelligence.

   3.3. TRACEABILITY AND AUTHORIZATION
   Any significant shift in weighting coefficients (e.g., changing Geographic or Volume weights) must be formally documented via a 'Decision Memo' and signed by a designated Director (EX-01) or higher.

4. DATA INTEGRITY AND STEWARDSHIP
Institutions are responsible for the 'Health' of their underlying data. Legacy artifacts (e.g., default date values from 20th-century migrations) must be identified, isolated, and documented via technical audit trails to prevent skewed risk assessments.

VANCOUVER MARITIME & LOGISTICS REVIEW
SPECIAL INDUSTRY REPORT | VOLUME 14, ISSUE 5
DATE: MAY 14, 2026
TOPIC: URBAN CORRIDOR CONGESTION AND DANGEROUS GOODS (DG) VOLUMES

[EXECUTIVE SUMMARY]
The Port of Vancouver and its associated rail transload hubs have recorded a historic 32% year-over-year increase in the movement of Class 3 (Flammable Liquids) and Class 8 (Corrosives) during the Q1-Q2 transition of 2026. This surge, driven by new Pacific export contracts and logistical rerouting from the U.S. West Coast, has created a critical density of dangerous goods within 50km of the Vancouver Census Metropolitan Area (CMA).

[REGIONAL PRESSURE POINTS]
Local safety advocates and regional oversight teams have identified several "Red Zones" where the proximity of DG storage to high-density residential areas poses an elevated risk. Specifically:
- The Burnaby Transload Corridor: Increase in unannounced Class 3 bulk storage.
- The North York Hub (Ontario-Pacific Link): Aging infrastructure handling higher-than-rated volumes.
- New Westminster Rail Yards: Increased dwell times for tank cars carrying corrosive substances.

[REGULATORY CRITICISM]
A significant portion of this report focuses on the perceived failure of the current "National Oversight Plan" (based on 2015 historical weighting) to adapt to these shifts. Industry experts argue that the existing 5% Geographic Sensitivity weight is "statistically deaf" to the reality of 2026 urban density.

"The current model treats a high-volume facility in a remote rural area with the same risk-weight as a similar facility in the heart of the Lower Mainland," says the report. "Without a drastic recalibration of geographic risk factors—potentially tripling the current weight—the department risks missing a catastrophic event in a densely populated urban corridor."

[LOGISTICAL DATA SNAPSHOT]
- Average Daily Tank Car Dwell Time: Increased from 14 hours to 26 hours.
- Class 3 (Flammable) Tonnage: 1.4M tons (up from 0.9M in 2025).
- Proximity Incident Rate: 2.1 minor leaks per 1,000 movements (trending upward).

[CONCLUSION]
The report concludes that federal oversight bodies must move away from "Volume-only" models and transition toward "Proximity-weighted" risk assessments before the 2026-27 fiscal cycle begins.

Legacy– PawPromise ™
Ensuring Continuity for the Family Members Who Can't Speak
Investor Presentation | Series Seed 2024

--- Slide 2 ---
Bridging the Continuity Gap
Legacy–PawPromise™ is a structured, actionable platform designed to connect pet owners with verified future caregivers. Our primary mission is to eradicate the uncertainty that millions of pets face when owners pass away or become incapacitated.
Strategic Objectives:
Reduce Shelter Intake:  Preventing pets from entering overburdened systems.
Actionable Directives:  Moving beyond vague verbal wishes to enforceable care plans.
Market Connectivity:  Creating a high-trust, two-sided marketplace.
MISSION & EXECUTIVE OVERVIEW

--- Slide 3 ---
The "Silent Crisis" in Pet Ownership
Most pet owners treat their pets as family, yet failed planning leads to tragic outcomes. Millions of pets are surrendered annually due to owner death or health crises because there was no accessible blueprint for their care.
The current reliance on "informal verbal agreements" (25% of owners) fails under legal or logistical pressure, leaving the pet's future entirely at risk.
52.5%
Owners lack any formal care plan
THE PROBLEM: STRUCTURAL UNCERTAINTY

--- Slide 4 ---
A Growing Canadian Demand
With  ~60% of Canadian households  owning at least one pet, the market for pet-related services is expanding rapidly. However, the true driver is the intersection of two major trends:
The Aging Population:  As the population ages, the demand for continuity planning and "peace-of-mind" services is reaching an all-time high.
Humanization of Pets:  36% of our survey respondents view pets as "family members," while 43% see them as "companions," signaling high emotional spending potential.
MARKET OPPORTUNITY & DEMOGRAPHICS

--- Slide 5 ---
Our research of 102 respondents reveals a high frequency of emotional concern but a critical lack of operational readiness. We are targeting the emotional urgency to drive platform adoption.

Nearly half of all respondents think about their pet's future "often," proving that the pain point is top-of-mind and persistent.
27%
Zero Readiness
Over a quarter of owners have absolutely no plan, formal or informal, representing a significant immediate capture market.
54%
Relationship Priority
A majority believe it is "extremely important" to build a relationship with future caregivers in advance, a key feature of our platform.
PLANNING BEHAVIORS & READINESS

--- Slide 6 ---
Incredible Market Pull
The survey data reflects an overwhelming mandate for our solution. The "intent to try" remains consistent across pet owners and non-owners alike.
Key Insight
"Demand is consistently high across both pet owners and non-owners, indicating broad market pull and a self-sustaining supply of caregivers (the Guardian side)."

94.1% Market Feasibility (Potential Guardians)
UNIVERSAL PLATFORM VALIDATION

--- Slide 7 ---
1. Personalized Care Plans
Digitized routines, food specifications, medications, and comfort behaviors to ensure continuity of experience.
2. Care Vault™ Storage
Secure repository for medical records, insurance, and legal designations accessible in any emergency.
3. Backup Designation Tools
Naming primary and backup contacts, integrated with our verified Guardian network.
PRODUCT ECOSYSTEM: THE CARE VAULT™

--- Slide 8 ---
Market demand is not limited to owners; animal lovers (non-owners) provide the necessary "supply side" for our two-sided marketplace.
Conclusion: Strong market pull across both segments ensures liquidity for the Caregiver marketplace.
COMPARATIVE SEGMENT ANALYSIS

--- Slide 9 ---
The No-Plan Segment (n=31)
This group represents our "Low-Hanging Fruit" for acquisition, exhibiting the highest urgency and willingness to pay.
Strategic Insight
The highest-need segment (those without a formal plan) shows the strongest product pull. We will target this urgency during the MVP launch to optimize conversion rates and Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC).
100% Platform Trial Interest

Investors frequently ask about trust. Our data identifies specific keywords that we will address through our product roadmap and communication strategy.

Strong Payment Signals
93.1% of all respondents  are willing to pay for the service. The data suggests a subscription-first model is highly viable.

One-time concierge services during pet relocation, providing logistical and emotional support for the transition.
Opportunities for insurance referrals, trust management, and long-term financial planning for pet care.

Advance Contract Award Notice
1. An ACAN is a public notice indicating to the supplier community that a department or agency
intends to award a contract for goods, services or construction to a pre-identified supplier, thereby
allowing other suppliers to signal their interest in bidding, by submitting a statement of
capabilities.
If no supplier submits a statement of capabilities that meets the requirements set out in the
ACAN, on or before the closing date stated in the ACAN, the contracting officer may then proceed
with the award to the pre-identified supplier.
2. Definition of the requirement
PSPC is involved in document capture and digitization, form processing, OCR and barcode
recognition, intelligent document recognition and categorization of unstructured documents.
PSPC supports the Digital Canada 150 initiative, a 2013 plan to guide federal departments and
agencies into the digital future. The mandate includes the digitization of government documents
and support for Open Government and the Accessible Canada Act.
In order to meet an increasingly-complex growing demand in a timely and effective manner,
PSPC has broadened its document imaging services to provide end-to-end managed services as
a Common Service provider to federal government departments and agencies by establishing
multi-vendor document imaging contracts. Also, PSPC is constantly looking forward to developing
new expertise related to document digitization in order to increase the variety and volume of the
imaged documents.
PSPC requires expertise and reports in order to maintain its successful position in the market. We
also need the reports to direct our actions and support our decisions related to the future of our
business. We want to have expert analysis to guide our business towards future needs and
industry trends.
The reports must include a complete coverage of the world capture software market, an expert
analysis of the market and comments on industry, trends, events and any other important
information on market development.
The work will involve the following:
In order to support PSPC’s business and to help plan activities on a short and long-term basis,
professional reports, sent in PDF format, are needed, detailing the market for document capture
software. The reports need to be used and consulted by at least three users in our organization.
The following relevant information is required in the reports:
• Projection of the total global market over a three-year period with details on the best
practices (how to acquire documents, information source, media type, etc.)
• Information on major vendors with details on their services and location, revenues,
strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, threats, expected direction.
• Sales made and planned for this actual fiscal year per major country/region including (but
not limited to): Canada, United States, and Europe.

PSPC is looking to have a one (1) contract year plus two (2) option years. The contractor must
offer, starting April 1st, 2026 as well as in each of the option years, the following deliverables:
1) These quarterly reports in pdf format via email:
a) Global market report and analysis for the current year
b) Industry major vendor report and analysis for the current year
c) Vertical market report and analysis for the current year
2) Up to eight (8) hours of phone counseling provided by an expert analyst
Optional deliverable in each year:
One optional day on-site visit (8 hours) by an expert analyst for strategy review and
market discussion
3. Criteria for assessment of the Statement of Capabilities (Minimum Essential
Requirements)
Any interested supplier must demonstrate by way of a statement of capabilities that it meets
the following requirements:
A. Experience;
1. The resource must have produced an annual analysis of the global
document imaging industry in each of the past 10 years
2. The resource must have a minimum of 10 years of experience in global
document imaging industry analysis
3. The resource must have a minimum of 15 years’ experience as an expert
analyst in the field of document imaging in the last 25 years.
B. Academic qualifications
1. The resource must have must possess, at a minimum, either
a. An undergraduate degree and 10 years of work experience
in the capture field, or
b. 15 years of experience in the capture field
C. Professional designation, accreditation, and/or certification
1. The resource must possess, at a minimum, one of the following:
a. vendor certifications or accreditations related to capture
products, or
b. work experience with the Association for Intelligent
Information Management (AIIM), or
c. accreditations with the Association for Intelligent Information
Management (AIIM), or
d. at least one (1) published articles or white papers on the
capture industry in the last 15 years.

Canadian Free Trade Agreement
Canada Korea Free Trade Agreement

Our business is all about digitalization; we need to have access to the best and latest analysis of
the document capture software market. InfoSource is renowned worldwide as being the premier
specialist company following the document capture market. We need access to two different
things. First one being the worldwide analysis and second one being the access to an expert
analyst for consultation.
Since our business is always evolving, we need to follow the trends and be up to date on what’s
coming up in the document capture software market. We have to stay competitive and be ready
to respond to our current and our future clients’ needs. InfoSource’s reports are recognized to be

Last but not least, having access to InfoSources expert for consultation and advice about our
strategy review and market-related concerns is strategically advantageous for us. The document

the analysis of this particular market.

Préavis d’adjudication de contrat
1. Un PAC est un avis public informant la collectivité des fournisseurs qu'un ministère ou organisme
a l'intention d'attribuer un contrat pour des biens, des services ou des travaux de construction à
un fournisseur sélectionné à l'avance, ce qui permet aux autres fournisseurs de signaler leur
intérêt à soumissionner en présentant un énoncé des capacités.
Si aucun fournisseur ne présente un énoncé des capacités qui satisfait aux exigences établies
dans le PAC, au plus tard à la date de clôture indiquée dans le PAC, l'agent de négociation des
contrats peut procéder à l'attribution du contrat au fournisseur sélectionné à l'avance.
2. Définition du besoin
SPAC participe à la capture et à la numérisation de documents, au traitement de formulaires, à la
reconnaissance optique des caractères et des codes-barres, à la reconnaissance intelligente de
documents et à la catégorisation de documents non structurés. SPAC appuie l’initiative Canada
numérique 150, un programme de 2013 visant à guider les ministères et les organismes fédéraux
dans l’avenir numérique. Le mandat comprend la numérisation de documents gouvernementaux
et le soutien à la Loi sur le gouvernement ouvert et à la Loi sur le Canada accessible.
Afin de répondre à une demande croissante de plus en plus complexe et ce, de manière rapide et
efficace, SPAC a élargi ses services d'imagerie documentaire afin de fournir des services gérés
du début à la fin en tant que fournisseur de services communs aux ministères et organismes
fédéraux en établissant des contrats d’imagerie à des fournisseurs multiples. De plus, SPAC
cherche constamment à développer une nouvelle expertise en numérisation de documents afin
d'accroître la variété et le volume des documents numérisés.
SPAC est à la recherche d’expertise et de rapports pour maintenir sa position sur le marché.
Nous avons également besoin de rapports pour orienter nos actions et appuyer nos décisions
relatives à l'avenir de notre entreprise. Nous voulons avoir une analyse experte pour guider notre
entreprise vers les besoins futurs et les tendances du secteur.
Les rapports doivent inclure une couverture complète du marché mondial des logiciels de
capture, une analyse experte du marché et des commentaires sur le secteur, les tendances, les
événements et toute autre information importante sur le développement du marché.
Le travail comporte les éléments suivants:
Afin de soutenir les activités de SPAC et d’aider à planifier les activités à court et long termes,
des rapports professionnels détaillant le marché des logiciels de capture de documents, envoyés
en format PDF, sont nécessaires. Les rapports doivent être utilisés et consultés par au moins
trois utilisateurs de notre organisation.
Les informations pertinentes suivantes sont requises dans les rapports:
• Projection du marché mondial total sur une période de trois (3) ans avec des détails sur
les meilleures pratiques (comment acquérir des documents, la source d'informations, le
type de support, etc.) ;
• Informations sur les principaux fournisseurs avec des détails sur leurs services et leur
emplacement, leurs revenus, leurs forces, leurs faiblesses, leurs opportunités, leurs
menaces et leur direction attendue ;

• Ventes réalisées et planifiées pour cette année fiscale par grand pays/région
comprenant, sans toutefois s'y limiter, le Canada, les États-Unis et l’Europe.
SPAC cherche à avoir une (1) année de contrat plus deux (2) années en option. L'entrepreneur
doit offrir, à compter du 1er avril 2026 ainsi que pour chacune des années en option, les produits
livrables suivants:
1) Ces rapports trimestriels en format PDF par courrier électronique :
a) Rapport et analyse du marché mondial pour l'année en cours ;
b) Rapport et analyse des principaux fournisseurs du secteur pour l'année en cours ;

Livrable en option chaque année:
Une visite optionnelle d'une journée (8 heures) sur le site par un expert analyste pour

aux exigences suivantes:
A. Expérience

de l'imagerie documentaire au cours de chacune des dix (10) dernières
années;
